title: Diagnosis and Management of Community-acquired Pneumonia: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
one_line_summary: This ATS guideline updates CAP management, addressing lung ultrasound for diagnosis, withholding antibiotics in viral CAP, short-course antibiotics, and adjunctive corticosteroids in severe CAP, aiming for individualized patient care.
key_pearls: Lung ultrasound (LUS) can be an acceptable alternative to chest radiography for CAP diagnosis if performed by trained clinicians.
For outpatients with viral CAP and no comorbidities, consider withholding empiric antibiotics, but prescribe for those with comorbidities.
For all inpatients with viral CAP (nonsevere or severe), initiate standard empiric antibacterial therapy due to high bacterial coinfection rates.
Short-course antibiotics (<5 days, minimum 3 days) are suggested for stable outpatients and nonsevere inpatients with CAP.
For severe CAP inpatients, a standard course of antibiotics (>=5 days) is strongly **recommended** due to high risk of treatment failure.
Do NOT administer systemic corticosteroids for nonsevere CAP.
Consider adjunctive systemic corticosteroids for severe CAP, but absolutely avoid them in influenza-associated CAP.
consensus_method: GRADE methodology and CORE consensus process
search_period: January 1, 1946, to March 31, 2023, with updates on November 27, 2024, and February 20, 2025

recommendation_blocks.topic: Lung Ultrasound versus Chest Radiography to Diagnose CAP
recommendation_blocks.narrative: Confirming the clinical suspicion of pneumonia with imaging is a cornerstone of CAP management. While chest radiography (CXR) is the historical standard, it has limited sensitivity compared to chest computed tomography (CT). However, CT is costly, exposes patients to ionizing radiation, and is unavailable in many resource-limited settings. Lung ultrasound (LUS) has emerged as a rapid, radiation-free, point-of-care alternative that can be performed at the bedside by trained clinicians. The panel identified 12 studies comparing LUS and CXR using chest CT as the reference standard. One study was excluded as an outlier due to 100% discordance. The remaining 11 studies (enrolling 939 patients) provided indirect evidence, as they represented a subset of patients with suspected CAP who required a CT scan for clinical reasons. LUS Diagnostic Performance: Median sensitivity of 95% (range, 68% to 100%); median specificity of 75% (range, 0% to 100%). CXR Diagnostic Performance: Median sensitivity of 70% (range, 16% to 94%); median specificity of 55% (range, 0% to 94%). Certainty of Evidence: Low (downgraded due to inconsistency and imprecision/wide confidence intervals). Consensus Vote: 13 of 15 committee members (87%) voted in favor of the recommendation. Key Sonographic Features of Pneumonia: Clinicians must identify consolidation (irregular marginal contour, air bronchograms, air trapping sign), vertical artifacts (B-lines), and pleural effusions. Expertise Requirements: LUS must be performed using a low-frequency probe (14 to 16 cm penetration in adults) following a standardized protocol evaluating **superior**/**inferior** portions of the anterior, lateral, and posterior chest wall. Images must be archived (2 to 4 second video loops) and formally documented.

recommendations.statement: Suggest lung ultrasound as an acceptable alternative to chest radiography for diagnosing CAP in trained clinicians.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Empiric Antibacterial Therapy for CAP with Positive Respiratory Virus Testing
recommendation_blocks.narrative: The clinical dilemma is whether identifying a respiratory virus via highly sensitive molecular assays (e.g., multiplex PCR) allows clinicians to safely withhold or discontinue empiric antibiotics. The primary concern is missing a bacterial-viral coinfection, which historically carries high **mortality** (as seen in the 1918 and 1957 influenza pandemics). Conversely, routine antibiotic use in pure viral infections drives resistance, increases costs, and exposes patients to adverse events. A systematic review of 3,895 articles identified zero comparative studies (antibiotics vs. no antibiotics in patients with CAP and a positive viral PCR). The panel had to rely on indirect epidemiologic data, pathophysiologic rationale, and clinical experience (very low-quality evidence). Coinfection Rates: In hospitalized patients with viral CAP (excluding SARS-CoV-2), bacterial pathogens are co-detected in 25% to 30% of cases. In patients hospitalized with RSV, bacterial coinfection occurs in 12% to 29%. During the COVID-19 pandemic, bacterial coinfection rates were lower (approximately 3.5% overall, rising to 10% in intubated patients, compared to 30% in intubated influenza patients). Biomarker Limitations: Procalcitonin (PCT) has poor sensitivity and specificity (at best 75% to 80%) for distinguishing bacterial from viral etiologies, and its performance is further degraded in the presence of viral infections. Comorbidities Warranting Antibiotics in Outpatients: High-agreement conditions include chronic pulmonary disease other than asthma (82% agreement), end-stage liver disease (71%), end-stage renal disease (65%), cardiovascular disease (53%), alcoholism (53%), and neoplastic disease (53%).

recommendations.statement: For outpatients with CAP and a positive respiratory virus test who do not have comorbidities, suggest NOT prescribing empiric antibiotics.
For outpatients with CAP and a positive respiratory virus test who have comorbidities, suggest prescribing empiric antibiotics.
For inpatients with nonsevere CAP and a positive respiratory virus test, suggest prescribing empiric antibiotics.
For inpatients with severe CAP and a positive respiratory virus test, suggest prescribing empiric antibiotics.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Historically, CAP guidelines **recommended** 7 to 14 days of therapy. Modern infectious disease principles suggest that once a patient achieves clinical stability, the host immune response can clear the remaining pathogen load, and continuing antibiotics only drives toxicity and resistance. The panel evaluated whether an "ultra-short" course (less than 5 days, with a minimum of 3 days) is noninferior to standard courses (5 or more days). The panel identified four randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing less than 5 effective days of therapy with 5 or more days. Two trials evaluated single-dose extended-release azithromycin in outpatients (effectively a 3-day course due to its long half-life of 3 days). Two trials evaluated beta-lactams in hospitalized patients (Moussaoui et al. and Dinh et al. [PTC trial]), where patients who achieved clinical stability after 3 days of therapy were randomized to placebo versus continued active treatment. Clinical Cure Rate (1 to 2 weeks): 85.6% in the short-duration group versus 87.6% in the longer-duration group (Risk Ratio [RR] 0.98; 95% CI, 0.91 to 1.05). Clinical Cure Rate (3 to 4 weeks): 81.0% in the short-duration group versus 82.5% in the longer-duration group (RR 0.99; 95% CI, 0.92 to 1.07). **Mortality**: No statistically **significant** difference (2.0% in short-course vs. 1.3% in long-course; RR 1.49; 95% CI, 0.25 to 8.79). Hospital Length of Stay (LOS): No **significant** difference (mean difference -0.35 days; 95% CI, -1.17 to 0.47 days). Certainty of Evidence: Low (due to risk of bias in unblinded trials and imprecision). Clinical Stability Criteria: Temperature <= 37.8 degrees C, Heart Rate < 100 beats/min, Respiratory Rate < 24 breaths/min, SpO2 >= 90% or PaO2 >= **60 mmHg** on room air (or return to baseline oxygen requirement), Systolic Blood Pressure >= **90 mmHg**, and normal mental status.

recommendations.statement: For outpatients with CAP reaching clinical stability, suggest less than 5 days (minimum 3 days) of antibiotics.
For inpatients with nonsevere CAP reaching clinical stability, suggest less than 5 days (minimum 3 days) of antibiotics.
For inpatients with severe CAP reaching clinical stability, suggest 5 or more days of antibiotics.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Should Adults Hospitalized with CAP Be Treated with Corticosteroids?
recommendation_blocks.narrative: The host inflammatory response to CAP can drive severe lung injury, ARDS, and multi-organ failure. Systemic corticosteroids act as potent immunomodulators to blunt this response. However, they carry **significant** risks, including secondary infections, impaired viral clearance, gastrointestinal bleeding, and severe hyperglycemia. The panel re-evaluated this question in light of recent landmark trials, including the CAPE COD trial (Dequin et al., evaluating hydrocortisone in severe CAP) and the RECOVERY trial (evaluating dexamethasone in COVID-19). The panel analyzed 15 RCTs enrolling hospitalized patients. Six trials evaluated hydrocortisone, three evaluated methylprednisolone, three evaluated dexamethasone, and three evaluated prednisone/prednisolone. Corticosteroid duration ranged from 5 days or fewer (7 trials) to 7 days (5 trials) or longer (3 trials). Overall **Mortality**: Corticosteroids **significantly** decreased **mortality** (6.1% vs. 9.1%; RR 0.68; 95% CI, 0.53 to 0.86). Number Needed to Treat (NNT) = 34. Nonsevere CAP **Mortality**: No statistically **significant** decrease (4.4% vs. 6.7%; RR 0.88; 95% CI, 0.55 to 1.41). Severe CAP **Mortality**: **Significant** decrease in **mortality** (9.8% vs. 15.1%; RR 0.62; 95% CI, 0.41 to 0.94). NNT = 17. Length of Stay: Decreased overall by 1.53 days (95% CI, -2.14 to -0.91 days). In severe CAP, the decrease was 1.06 days (95% CI, -2.01 to -0.12 days). In nonsevere CAP, the decrease was not statistically **significant** (-0.52 days; 95% CI, -1.33 to 0.28 days). Adverse Events: No statistically **significant** increase in overall serious adverse events (RR 1.2; 95% CI, 0.89 to 1.63), though individual trials reported high rates of hyperglycemia requiring insulin therapy. Certainty of Evidence: Low (due to inconsistency of results across trials, such as the positive CAPE COD trial vs. the neutral ESCAPe trial).

recommendations.statement: For inpatients with nonsevere CAP, recommend NOT administering systemic corticosteroids.
For inpatients with severe CAP, suggest administering systemic corticosteroids, excluding patients with severe CAP caused by influenza.

bedside_protocol.title: Point-of-Care Lung Ultrasound (POCUS) for Suspected CAP
bedside_protocol.action: 1. Clinical Scenario: Adult patient presents to the Emergency Department or ICU with acute cough, fever, dyspnea, and clinical suspicion of CAP. 2. Expertise Check: Confirm the performing clinician meets Table 4 criteria (formal training, standardized multi-zone protocol, image archiving capability). 3. Scanning Protocol: Perform a 12-zone thoracic scan (bilateral anterior, lateral, and posterior zones, superior and inferior fields). 4. Diagnostic Decision Tree: - If consolidation (irregular margins, air bronchograms) or focal B-lines are present: Confirm CAP diagnosis. Initiate appropriate management. - If LUS is negative but clinical suspicion remains high: Obtain a standard chest radiograph. If CXR is negative but suspicion remains extremely high, proceed to chest CT. - If barriers to LUS exist (obesity, extensive surgical scars, chest tubes): Default immediately to chest radiography.

bedside_protocol.title: Antibiotic Stewardship in Viral CAP
bedside_protocol.action: 1. Clinical Scenario: Patient presents with clinical and radiographic evidence of CAP and has a positive multiplex PCR for a respiratory virus (e.g., Influenza, RSV, Adenovirus). 2. Step 1: Determine Care Setting & Severity: - If Severe CAP (Table 2) or requiring hospitalization: Do not withhold antibiotics. Initiate standard empiric antibacterial therapy immediately. - If Outpatient: Proceed to Step 3. 3. Step 3: Assess Comorbidities (Table 5): - If patient has any of the following: COPD, bronchiectasis, interstitial lung disease, end-stage liver disease, end-stage renal disease, congestive heart failure, active malignancy, or chronic alcoholism -> Prescribe empiric antibiotics. - If patient is young, healthy, and has no significant comorbidities: Withhold empiric antibiotics. Provide supportive care, antiviral therapy if indicated (e.g., oseltamivir), and establish a strict 24-hour clinical follow-up plan.

bedside_protocol.title: Short-Course Antibiotic Titration (The 3-Day Rule)
bedside_protocol.action: 1. Clinical Scenario: Patient hospitalized with nonsevere CAP on empiric beta-lactam/macrolide or fluoroquinolone therapy. 2. Daily Assessment (Starting at 48 Hours): Assess for clinical stability using Table 6 criteria: - Temperature < 37.8 degrees C - Heart Rate < 100 beats/min - Respiratory Rate < 24 breaths/min - SpO2 >= 90% on room air (or return to baseline oxygen needs) - Systolic Blood Pressure >= 90 mmHg - Normal mental status - Ability to maintain oral intake. 3. Decision Tree at Day 3 (72 Hours): - If all stability criteria are met AND there are no complicating factors: Discontinue antibiotics on Day 3. - If stability criteria are not met: Continue antibiotics and reassess daily. Discontinue 48 hours after stability is achieved. - Absolute Exclusion Criteria (Require >= 5 to 7 days): Confirmed infection with Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Legionella pneumophila, or other intracellular pathogens; radiographic evidence of necrotizing pneumonia, lung abscess, or empyema; severe chronic lung disease (bronchiectasis); or initial presentation with severe CAP.

bedside_protocol.title: Corticosteroid Administration in Severe CAP
bedside_protocol.action: 1. Clinical Scenario: Patient hospitalized with severe CAP defined by Table 2 (requires 1 major criterion [mechanical ventilation, vasopressors] or >= 3 minor criteria [RR >= 30, PaO2/FiO2 ratio < 250, multilobar infiltrates, confusion, BUN >= 20 mg/dL, WBC < 4000, Platelets < 100,000, Temp < 36 degrees C, hypotension]). 2. Step 1: Screen for Contraindications: - Absolute Contraindications: Positive test for Influenza, active Aspergillus infection, uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, active gastrointestinal bleeding, pregnancy. 3. Step 2: Biomarker and Timing Check: - Ideal candidates are within 24 hours of admission, have severe respiratory failure (PaO2/FiO2 ratio < 300), and elevated inflammatory markers (CRP > 150 mg/L). 4. Step 3: Dosing Regimen (Based on CAPE COD Protocol): - Initiate Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day via continuous intravenous infusion (or 50 mg IV every 6 hours). - Duration: Administer for 4 to 7 days. Upon clinical improvement, taper the dose over a total of 8 to 14 days. - Monitoring: Check blood glucose every 6 hours. Initiate insulin therapy for any blood glucose > 180 mg/dL. Monitor closely for secondary nosocomial infections.

strengths_limitations: Strengths: The guideline utilized a rigorous GRADE methodology and CORE consensus process, ensuring transparent recommendations. It formally integrated patient input, emphasizing patient convenience and concerns about radiation/antibiotic side effects. Recommendations are nuanced, stratifying patients by severity and care setting. Limitations: The guideline was not approved by the IDSA, reflecting deep professional disagreement on key recommendations (withholding antibiotics in viral CAP, short-course antibiotics). Most major recommendations are based on low or very low-quality evidence, with diagnostic accuracy of LUS derived from selected populations and viral CAP recommendations based on indirect epidemiologic data. The definition of severe CAP is heterogeneous, potentially affecting corticosteroid benefits.
